segments.title: Why the G3's third turret troubles this design
segments.summary: The G3's amidships third turret illustrates the tension between compact engineering and a battleship's usable arcs of fire.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that the third G3 turret is hemmed in by the superstructure and secondary battery, limiting its ability to engage targets ahead. He accepts that the arrangement has engineering logic, but judges the combat value too low for the weight and cost it consumes. He contrasts it with the French choice to concentrate two quadruple turrets forward, which at least gives the carried guns a clear tactical purpose. The comparison leads him toward layouts that give every heavy turret a more useful field of fire.

evidence.note: He critiques the G3 third turret's restricted forward firing arc and combat utility.

segments.title: N3's 18-inch gun and the Agincourt design hypothesis
segments.summary: The N3's heavy armament leads into a research hypothesis that a proposed HMS Agincourt would have resembled an 18-inch-gunned Queen Elizabeth class.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke separates his dislike of the N3 layout from his interest in its 18-inch gun. He uses that calibre to introduce his working hypothesis about a proposed HMS Agincourt design. In his reconstruction, Agincourt may have been conceived as something like a Queen Elizabeth-class fast battleship armed with 18-inch weapons. He carefully presents this as research in progress for a paper rather than as an established design history.

evidence.note: He links the N3's 18-inch armament to his provisional Agincourt-as-18-inch-Queen-Elizabeth research hypothesis.

segments.title: What a naval game can test that a drawing cannot
segments.summary: Ultimate Admiral: Dreadnoughts serves as a design sandbox for exploring displacement, manoeuvre, and combat, while remaining a simulation rather than historical proof.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke explains why he uses Ultimate Admiral: Dreadnoughts alongside more conventional design tools. CAD can produce a drawing, but the game also imposes displacement constraints and lets a concept manoeuvre and fight at sea. Those functions make it useful for asking whether an attractive layout remains workable as a complete ship. The later comparison between game fittings and a plausible 1920s fit shows why the simulation's results still need historical judgment.

evidence.note: He contrasts CAD with a game that can test displacement, operational manoeuvre, and combat performance.

segments.title: The cost of a quadruple-turret G3 hull
segments.summary: A visually attractive quadruple-turret concept exposes how quickly a G3-derived hull can become impractically heavy.
segments.body: The first game design grew to roughly 80,000 tons once its desired armament and fittings were included. Dr. Clarke treats that figure as excessive for a G3-derived hull even though he likes the quadruple-turret version's appearance. He instead presents a three-twin-16-inch arrangement, using the exercise to show how displacement limits discipline a designer's ambitions. The choice is less about a single ideal calibre than about keeping the whole ship workable.

evidence.note: He contrasts an 80,000-ton quadruple-turret version with a three-twin-16-inch alternative.

segments.title: Turret arcs, armour layout, and damage resilience
segments.summary: Widely usable turret arcs can justify a longer hull, while gunhouse placement also affects how blast and flooding loads travel through it.
segments.body: The proposed arrangement lets each main turret rotate through a full circle, which Dr. Clarke sees as valuable in a confused close-range action. A turret can train toward a new threat by the shorter route instead of rotating almost completely around the ship. He acknowledges that spreading the armament lengthens the hull and reduces the concentration of heavy weapons. He also links large armoured turret structures and tight internal subdivision to the way torpedo damage energy may be concentrated rather than dissipated through the hull.

evidence.note: The discussion connects 360-degree turret training, ship length, and torpedo-damage effects.

segments.title: A 1920s six-inch dual-purpose secondary battery
segments.summary: Twin six-inch mounts become a design argument for investing early in a high-angle secondary battery rather than accepting later compromises.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke selects twin six-inch turrets comparable to contemporary cruiser fittings so the capital ship can share an established cruiser mounting. He imagines adapting the high-elevation principle used in British eight-inch cruiser turrets to a smaller weapon. A six-inch gun able to reach 70 degrees could serve as a more capable dual-purpose battery if developed in the 1920s. The counterfactual is framed as one route that might reduce the need for the later Dido-style solution.

evidence.note: He explains his choice of twin six-inch turrets and the appeal of a 70-degree dual-purpose mount.

segments.title: Separating game fittings from a plausible 1920s ship
segments.summary: A combat-game design carries radar, sonar, and advanced machinery that make sense in play but distort a 1920s displacement calculation.
segments.body: The fully equipped version displaces roughly 70,000 tons after allowing for a long high-speed range, heavy protection, detection equipment, and advanced gun machinery. Dr. Clarke stresses that radar, sonar, autoloaders, and electro-hydraulic turrets are game-era necessities rather than an honest early-1920s fit. The game's very long range figure at 28 knots also overstates what the historical requirement needs to provide. Removing those assumptions is the first step toward turning an entertaining design into a historical counterfactual.

evidence.note: He identifies the game-only fittings that add weight before asking what could be built in reality.

segments.title: Moving the secondary battery to test its firing arcs
segments.summary: Repeatedly moving the forward six-inch turrets demonstrates how a design tool can expose the tradeoff between appearance and useful aft fire.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke shows that the secondary-battery layout changed as he tested different positions. Moving the forward six-inch turrets outward widened their arcs and could bring twelve six-inch guns to bear astern. The arrangement nevertheless looked awkward, so he moved the mounts back in the next version. The iteration captures a recurring warship-design tension between a theoretically stronger firing arc and a layout that works convincingly as a whole.

evidence.note: He moves the forward six-inch turrets to gain wider arcs and twelve-gun aft fire, then restores their earlier position.

segments.title: Combining Hood's reach with Renown's cruising speed
segments.summary: The realistic concept is defined by a concrete operational requirement: roughly Hood's range while cruising at Renown's faster speed.
segments.body: The revised design is assessed at about 56,000 to 57,000 tons full load, rather than by the game's optimistic range figure. Its intended 8,000-nautical-mile range at 18 knots matches Hood's reach while improving on Hood's cruising speed. It also uses the Renown class's 18-knot cruising pace while seeking twice its stated endurance. This turns speed and range from brochure figures into a fleet-use requirement for a fast battleship.

evidence.note: He derives the concept's displacement and operating range from Hood and Renown comparisons.

segments.title: Why a 1920s hull grows heavier by the Second World War
segments.summary: Protection, auxiliary machinery, and later anti-aircraft fittings explain why a plausible early fast battleship gains substantial weight over time.
segments.body: The realistic version keeps torpedo defence, reinforced subdivision, conventional loading gear, and a Hood-like power plant, but leaves out radar and sonar in its early fit. Dr. Clarke notes that auxiliary machinery, accommodation, anti-aircraft weapons, and their crews all consume weight. He then trims belt and deck protection while preserving turret armour, presenting a ship equipped for Second World War service rather than frozen at launch. The design illustrates the cumulative displacement pressure created by modernization rather than a single dramatic change.

evidence.note: He contrasts the stripped early fit with wartime fittings and revised armour thicknesses.

segments.title: The British preference for a minimal conning tower
segments.summary: Reducing the conning tower to six inches of protection illustrates a British willingness to save weight on a heavily armoured command position.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke tries to follow the British practice of minimizing or deleting the heavily armoured conning tower. The game requires some structure to remain, so he reduces its armour to six inches. At that thickness it offers little of the traditional armoured citadel's protection and may simply consume displacement. The example shows how national command practices can shape which protected spaces a battleship designer considers worth their weight.

evidence.note: He follows British practice by minimizing the conning tower and questions the value of the remaining six-inch protection.

segments.title: A general-purpose battleship is not a duel-only design
segments.summary: The secondary battery and anti-aircraft outfit make sense in a balanced fleet action, even if a battleship-only duel rewards heavier main guns.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke notes that a game restricted to battleship duels encourages designers to discard secondary armament and seek the largest or fastest-firing main battery possible. Under those rules he would probably choose four quadruple 16-inch turrets. A more realistic task-group fight includes cruisers and destroyers, giving the six-inch and pom-pom batteries a distinct purpose. He links this balance to the Queen Elizabeth class as a successful model of the broadly useful fast battleship.

evidence.note: He contrasts a duel-optimized main battery with a general-purpose ship intended for fleet action.

segments.title: The Queen Elizabeth class as an unrealized fast-battleship idea
segments.summary: Higher-powered boilers could have made the Queen Elizabeth class a far more disruptive 28-knot fast-battleship proposition.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the Queen Elizabeths as the first fast battleships, a step beyond the earlier 13.5-inch-gunned super-dreadnoughts. He argues that contemporary technology was not developed as fully as it might have been. Small-tube boilers and extra power could, in his view, have pushed the class toward 28 knots. That potential informs the speed requirement of his own early-1920s concept.

evidence.note: He argues that more developed boiler technology could have given the Queen Elizabeth class 28 knots.

segments.title: G3, N3, and the blurred battlecruiser label
segments.summary: The G3 and N3 concepts reveal how a fast battleship combines battlecruiser mobility with enough armour for a fleet engagement.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sees the older contrast between commerce-hunting battlecruisers and battle-fighting battleships as too rigid for the G3 and N3 era. Most naval work demands mobility, while the rarer fleet action still demands a ship that can survive serious gunfire. He argues that the battlecruiser's reputation after Jutland also encouraged navies to prefer the fast-battleship label. His definition of the type is therefore a battlecruiser concept with the armour needed for that wider role.

evidence.note: He describes fast battleships as a hybrid of battlecruiser mobility and battleship protection, with Jutland shaping the preferred label.

segments.title: Would the Iowa class look like battlecruisers beside Montana?
segments.summary: An Iowa-Montana counterfactual shows that labels such as battlecruiser and fast battleship depend partly on the fleet context around a ship.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke asks how the Iowa class might have been described if the more heavily armed and protected Montana class had entered service. In that fleet, the Iowas' combination of speed and relative protection might have made them look like battlecruisers beside the Montanas. He uses the same question for the G3 designs as their reputation evolved toward the Second World War. The comparison shows that warship classification is relational as well as technical: a ship's label depends on doctrine, historical memory, and the other capital ships available.

evidence.note: He asks whether completed Montanas would have made the Iowas battlecruisers and applies the classification problem to G3.

segments.title: Testing the design against a Regia Marina-style force
segments.summary: A Mediterranean-style game battle tests the design as part of a mixed force, not as an isolated capital ship.
segments.body: The combat test pairs the proposed battleship, modified Town-class cruisers, and Tribal-class destroyers against an Italian force with a battleship and cruisers. Dr. Clarke chooses the Regia Marina because its early-war surface doctrine fits a carrier-less Mediterranean comparison better than a Japanese scenario. He cautions that the game makes ships fight until they sink and does not model ammunition exhaustion, so the result is not a reconstruction of real fleet behaviour. Within those limits, the six-inch battery deters enemy light cruisers while torpedoes help the British cruisers survive a close fight with Italian heavy cruisers.

evidence.note: He describes a game test against an Italian surface force and its mixed cruiser-destroyer setting.

segments.title: A shorter armour belt as the final compromise
segments.summary: Bringing the guns closer together shortens the protected length, accepting some firing-arc limits to release weight and growth margin.
segments.body: The final version is slightly shorter because its main turrets are brought closer together and the armoured belt is reduced in length. B turret retains a full circle of training, but the other positions sacrifice some arcs. Dr. Clarke considers that an acceptable compromise rather than his ideal arrangement. The hull still retains space and capacity for later machinery and equipment improvements without abandoning the core fast-battleship idea.

evidence.note: He explains the reduced armoured-belt length and the firing-arc compromise in the final design.
